project.name: My AI Chatbot
project.philosophy: Guided discovery — students fill in a pre-built template to feel the magic of talking to a real AI without getting lost in syntax.

architecture.overview: Two-layer system: a teacher-controlled backend proxy (hidden) and a student-facing frontend (their canvas). Students never interact with the backend directly.

frontend.language: HTML + CSS only (no JS written by students)
frontend.hosting: School-managed Glitch project — students access via shared URL
frontend.take_home: Students bookmark the school Glitch URL — no local copy needed

responsibilities: Store and inject OpenRouter API key (never exposed to frontend)
Run content filter on all outgoing requests and incoming responses
Rate limit requests per session to prevent abuse

system_prompt.example: You are a friendly coding helper for elementary school students aged 8-11. Only answer questions about computers, coding, and technology. Keep all answers short, simple, and encouraging. Never discuss anything unrelated to computers or coding. If asked off-topic questions, gently redirect back to coding topics.

content_filtering.replacement_message: Hmm, I can only talk about coding and computers! Try asking me something about that.

sessions.title: What is a Chatbot?
objectives: Understand what AI is at a high level
Understand the flow: user types -> internet -> AI -> response
Connect chatbots to things students already know (Siri, Google)
activities: Draw the data flow on whiteboard as a class
Play 'robot telephone' — one student is the AI, others send written questions
Discuss: what CAN'T a chatbot do?

sessions.title: Building the Face of Your Chatbot (HTML)
objectives: Understand what HTML tags are (the box-and-label analogy)
Fill in the chatbot name and welcome message
Identify the parts of the pre-built interface
student_tasks: Fill in: chatbot name between <title> tags
Fill in: welcome message in the designated span
Observe: the message box, input field, and send button already exist
sessions.teacher_notes: Use the analogy: HTML is like LEGO instructions — the pieces are already made, you just decide what to label them.

sessions.title: Making It Yours (CSS Colours & Style)
objectives: Understand that CSS controls how things look
Change colours using pre-defined CSS variables
See the immediate visual feedback of their changes
student_tasks: Change --primary-color variable to their favourite colour
Optional: change font-size of the chat bubbles
sessions.teacher_notes: Pre-define CSS variables at the top of the stylesheet so students only change one value per property. No cascading confusion.

sessions.title: Talk to Your Chatbot!
objectives: Experience sending a message and receiving an AI response
Understand at a high level that their message travelled to a server
Reflect on what the AI can and cannot do
activities: Teacher enables live backend — students visit the shared URL
Each student types their first message
Class discussion: What surprised you? What did it get wrong?
Optional: students try to 'break' the chatbot (good learning moment)
sessions.teacher_notes: The guardrails demonstration is a great teaching moment — when the AI redirects off-topic questions, explain WHY that happens.

stretch_goals: Add an emoji to the chatbot name
Write a longer, more creative welcome message
Try different colour combinations and screenshot your favourite
Draw on paper what you imagine the AI 'looks like' inside a computer

learning_outcomes: Can explain what an AI chatbot is in their own words
Understands that HTML controls structure and CSS controls appearance
Has successfully interacted with a real AI model
Understands that code can send and receive information over the internet
Knows that AI has rules and guardrails built in by people
